title: No Shame; No Shame; No One Has Any Shame!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; revenue percentages, Rule 110 references and financial figures have been checked against the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Reports about the Tamil Nadu Government's financial condition have continued to appear. Newspapers and weeklies have written about it at length. Yet the Government has offered neither explanation nor reply. On 31 December 2014, The Hindu published a report under the headline “TN facing financial crunch: CM admits to limited sources of revenue.” More telling than newspaper reports is Chief Minister Panneerselvam's own letter to the Prime Minister last month: “Tamil Nadu is already caught in a severe financial crisis. Given that the State Government has only limited revenue resources, it would be extremely difficult for it to bear any additional financial burden.”
The Hindu wrote: “It's official and out in the open. The State Government's financial health is quite bad. It was revealed in an innocuous statement by Chief Minister O. Panneerselvam.”

At the start of the 2014-15 financial year, the Tamil Nadu Government expected a revenue surplus of Rs 289 crore. In the Budget he presented on 13 February 2014, today's Chief Minister and then Finance Minister O. Panneerselvam stated on page 68: “The revenue surplus in 2014-15 will be Rs 289.36 crore.” His estimate proved wrong. At the end of the year's first six months, Tamil Nadu's revenue deficit stood at Rs 7,652 crore, according to The Hindu.

Tax revenue ordinarily accounts for more than two-thirds of total Government revenue. But the Government's performance from April to September 2014 shows that expected revenue did not arrive. Against an estimate of Rs 91,835 crore in the State's own tax revenue, only Rs 37,056 crore had been collected during that period. Statistics for each tax category show that no department earned anything close to the expected amount.

State revenue was expected to grow by 20.3 per cent, but grew only 14.6 per cent. Revenue from the State's share of Central taxes and Union allocations was expected to grow by forty-nine per cent, but grew only 37.68 per cent. Commercial-tax revenue was expected to grow 23.35 per cent, but rose just 1.48 per cent. State excise was expected to grow 28.75 per cent, but rose only 10.87 per cent. Revenue from registration and stamp duty, expected to grow 26.89 per cent, instead declined by 2.49 per cent. Motor-vehicle tax, expected to grow 39.71 per cent, rose only 2.73 per cent. The Hindu set this out in detail.

A senior official himself acknowledged: “The performance in respect of excise and registration is disappointing.” The Hindu concluded its report by stripping bare the Government's financial condition:

“The early signs of the declining financial health were visible even during the previous year (2013-2014) when the State registered a revenue deficit of about Rs. 1,790 crore, as per supplementary accounts. The revenue deficit came after two successive years of revenue surplus.”
This week's *Junior Vikatan* also carried an explanatory article by S.A.M. Barakkath Ali under the title “Tamil Nadu Government in financial crisis.” It quoted Finance Department circles as saying:

“The Finance Department is stumbling without meeting any target. The Government showed its greatest concern in increasing TASMAC revenue. Had it displayed that degree of administrative skill in other departments, total revenue would have increased. Government lethargy is responsible. The next Budget will be presented next month. Tax revenue will then fall short by Rs 18,000 crore. The Government has no option but to borrow. Tamil Nadu is already struggling under debt of Rs 1.80 lakh crore, and the State's overall growth will suffer.

“The Government has taken no firm action to collect old tax arrears or prevent evasion. Apart from State Government debt, the Electricity Board owes Rs 1.5 lakh crore. If the debts of public-sector undertakings are included, Tamil Nadu's total burden will exceed Rs 4 lakh crore.”

Finance Department officials reportedly told the journalist: “O. Panneerselvam finds a Tamil poet and keeps him nearby to prepare Assembly praise such as, ‘Guardian deity of the Tamil race! Lion of self-respect! Gita of Srirangam! Three-letter mantra! Sacrifice incarnate! Queen among women!’ He should spare a little concern for the Government's finances too.”

The article ended by asking: “There is an adviser above the Chief Secretary, one above the DGP, another in the Public Works Department, and above everyone a ‘People's Chief Minister.’ Despite so many masters, did no one advise them how to lift Tamil Nadu's finances?

“When will they implement the schemes announced in past Budgets: the Rs 3,833.62-crore Cooum restoration; Rs 5,166 crore for river-linking and flood prevention; the Rs 8,350-crore monorail along three corridors; the Rs 1,075-crore second outer ring road linking Nemilichery and Minjur; an integrated knowledge park at Chennai's DPI campus; and new one-TMC reservoirs at Thervaikandigai, Thirukandalam and Ramanjeri? Where will the money come from? As Panneerselvam says, ‘The man who cannot cut has fifty-eight sickles at his waist.’ Whom do announcements without financial resources seek to deceive? Did they imagine they could pound false grain and cook a Pongal feast?”

Udanpirappē, the proud distinction of bankrupting the Tamil Nadu Government and pushing its finances into the street within three and a half years of taking office belongs to Jayalalithaa's party! For fifteen months this Government failed to conclude the wage-revision agreement for transport workers, and came forward to talk only after they began a strike.
Why? Tamil Nadu Government employees receive a Pongal bonus every year. With only one week until the Tamil festival of Pongal, the Government must immediately announce that bonus.

When Jayalalithaa was Chief Minister and the Assembly was summoned, the first thing one remembered was her statement under Rule 110. Jayalalithaa alone read every such statement. Did she ever permit another Minister to read an announcement about that Minister's own department? Did the entire Tamil Nadu Government belong only to the Chief Minister?

Over three and a half years, Jayalalithaa read 139 Rule 110 statements. Chief Minister Panneerselvam himself told the Assembly that in 2013-14 she announced 236 schemes under Rule 110, but Government Orders were issued for only 116. We learned from him that no orders were issued for the remaining 120 schemes. He also said that even among all 236, work had been completed on only five. Thus 231 schemes announced that year remain incomplete. They remain incomplete because there is no financial resource for them.

As I explained earlier, do you know the total value of schemes announced in Jayalalithaa's Rule 110 statements over the past four years? Rs 1,27,500 crore and Rs 93 lakh. Do you know the amount listed as the Tamil Nadu Government's annual revenue receipts in last year's Budget? Rs 1,27,389 crore and Rs 89 lakh.

If Jayalalithaa announced through Rule 110 alone schemes equal in value to an entire year's revenue receipts, what was going on, sir? Was it a farce? Is it enough to announce schemes merely so that an announcement appears under her name in the newspapers every day? Should no one ask whether money exists? Can a Government act casually on the assumption, “Who will remember and question us?” If there is now no money even to pay bonuses to employees who work every day, whose fault is it?
On 10 December 2014, I cited several Rule 110 schemes and asked whether they had been completed. Could this Government offer any answer?

What is happening under this rule? Jayalalithaa, who was Chief Minister, was convicted by a court and went home. Does any Minister give the present Chief Minister Panneerselvam the respect due to that office? Is this not a Government of corruption everywhere, corruption in everything, corruption every day, corruption in all things?

How many Ministers have they themselves acted against over the past three and a half years? Those actions were taken because the Chief Minister understood that they had done wrong, were they not? Can that be denied? Has any other Government acted against so many of its own Ministers? Were the offences they committed publicly disclosed? Can the present Chief Minister now act against any Minister in the same way? His own position is in question.

How many corruption charges face this Government? Is the Aavin milk-adulteration scandal not enough? Milk prices increased, electricity tariffs increased, bus fares increased, and no procurement price was announced for sugarcane. When a judge was appointed to inquire into granite corruption, how many obstacles were placed before him? What became of the reported corruption in buying eggs for children in the nutritious-meal scheme? Where is the answer to reports that rules were violated in procuring pulses?

Are there any new factories? What does it mean when Chief Ministers of other States come to Tamil Nadu and invite industrialists here to move? How many more factories like Nokia and Foxconn have closed? Is that a sign of industrial development? What became of all the “Vision” schemes? Newspapers reported charges and possible action against the Mayor of Chennai. What happened? Was the report false?

Jayalalithaa was convicted by a court and remains at home on bail while her appeal is heard. Is it lawful for serving Government officials to visit her and hold consultations? Do we not need deliverance from all this?

Why was the annual November-December conference of District Collectors and police officers not held this year? The Chief Minister announced so many measures at last year's Collectors' conference. What became of them? What is the answer to the demand for a White Paper?

However one looks at it, the unshakeable truth is that within three and a half years the AIADMK Government has ruined and bankrupted Tamil Nadu's finances, destroyed the trust of the people who voted for it, and deceived them. No shame; no shame; no one in this has any shame!
With affection,
M.K.
9 January 2015
